replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Macdonald is one of the two most influential fantasy authors that nobody reads any more. (The other is William Morris.) He was a Scots clergyman and author whose first congregation cut his salary in half because he didn't spend enough of his time yelling about eternal damnation for their taste. He's the guy who convinced his friend Rev. Charles Dodgson to publish a charming little story he'd written for children; Dodgson decided to use the pen name Lewis Carroll, and yes, the story is Alice in Wonderland. Every significant figure in 19th and early 20th century fantasy read Macdonald and learned from him. Among the authors who have credited him as a major influence on their work are W. H. Auden, J. M. Barrie, Lord Dunsany, Mark Twain, L. Frank Baum, T. H. White, Richard Adams, Lloyd Alexander, Hilaire Belloc, G. K. Chesterton, Flannery O'Connor, George Orwell, Aldous Huxley, Ray Bradbury, C. S. Lewis, J. R. R. Tolkien, Walter de la Mare, E. Nesbit, Peter S. Beagle, Elizabeth Goudge, Brian Jacques, Neil Gaiman and Madeleine L'Engle.

I don't really have a nickel tour to offer; his fantasies include the two very fine children's novels you've cited, another children's novel titled At the Back of the North Wind, the adult fantasy novels Lilith and Phantastes, and a great many short pieces, which can be found in The Complete Fairy Tales of George Macdonald and other collections. He also wrote a couple of dozen straight novels which as far as I know nobody reads any more -- they're perfectly respectable Victorian novels and, um, rather dull. I know he also published volumes of Christian theology and sermons. Every single word he ever wrote is out of copyright, and you can download 73 books by him from Project Gutenberg -- and you could do much, much worse.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Good old George, I've been a fan for over three decades since I picked a copy of Lilith from my local library back when I lived in Spain...

MacDonald is, no doubt, a Christian theologian and mystic first and foremost. But he is also an incredible mythopoetic visionary. I think I've said it here before but, what strikes me the most of his fantasy work, is that the scenery and images look and feel very, very much like what nowadays could be call "Empowered Visualization" or "Pathworkings", and I thoroughly recommend reading in this light some of his fairy tales (like The Golden Key) as well as Phantastes and Lilith.

I've read biographies and some of his letters and I haven't come accross any Occult connection beyond an affinity with Swedenborg and Jacob Boehme. Funnily, there's this letter from Louisa Powell, then MacDonald's fiancee and later wife, writing to MacDonald as he was studying chemistry in Aberdeen:

"I am so glad, dearest George, that you're really going to begin the wonders of your Cabalistic art - I hope at least if you discover the Rosicrucian secret of prolonging life you will find out it is more glorious to die than to live on, desolate, alone and not caring for anything around you... I shall not be afraid of the magic of your bottles and crucibles and liquids and essences and shall only be too delighted to be admitted into your mystic chamber or laboratory sometimes."

Tongue in cheek, no doubt, but one always wonders...

Incidentally, RJ Stewart includes him in passim in his list of Justified Men, those humans who joined the Underworld and Faery realms after death.

Manuel

I had no idea about his mystical leanings nor his wide influence. When I was a kid, I checked out every single book in the school library with "goblin," "monster," or other fantasy words in the title that they had. There was one with some illustrations that really stuck with me, but when I wanted to go figure out what it was, I couldn't find it.

Well, every so often, the urge to know "what was that kid's book with the great goblin pictures?" would come up, and I'd dig around, but still no dice. When image searching became a thing, I thought it'd be easily done, but alas, no such luck.

Finally, that same old itch came back about 6 years ago, and deep into an abebooks search, I saw a cover that maybe, just maybe rang a bell, but searching for pictures by the artist didn't confirm for me, so I ordered the book, hoping against hope that I could finally know.

As luck would have it, it was exactly the right book! As I looked through the illustrations, the experience of pouring over them in the elementary school library came back to me vividly, and I was as thrilled to have a book full of such lovely artwork as I was to finally have settled something that had been bugging me for decades.

You've no doubt guessed by now that the book in question was The Princess and the Goblin. Specifically, the version abridged by Oliver Hunkin, and illustrated in pen and watercolor by Alan Perry. Unfortunately, as far as I can tell, this seems to be the only book he illustrated.

I suppose I'll have to give MacDonald's full, unabridged works a more serious look!

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: animal magnetism

You're quite correct. If you read Mesmer's original writings, you'll find that he was emphatically not talking about what we now call "hypnosis" -- that was invented by a Scotsman named James Braid, who took one offshoot of Mesmer's work, redefined the theory, and marketed it heavily. Mesmer's own work focused entirely on the etheric body and its psychological dimensions were a sideline. Here's a post I made on the subject:

It seems pretty doubtful to me that that kind of malaise would be coming from either your transient persona in this lifetime or from your soul, both of which are here specifically to experience life in a body, with all its wonders, limitations, and afflictions. Instead, that sounds much more like the work of energetic parasites and curses. If your karma saw a need for you to learn a particularly potent rebalancing lesson from this incarnation, it may have found an arrangement where you would have gotten seriously dumped on while still in utero. I have some experience with that particular kind of karmic debt, so I know that it can feel like it must predate this incarnation, given how early it began draining energies away.

Whatever the source of the parasitism, it does not have to be a permanent state. Your karma set you in a particular constellation of influences so that you could learn essential lessons that you had so-far missed on the previous go rounds. Once those lessons get learned, your karma will then offer you up new sets of lessons, often diametrically opposed to whatever the previous set was. That will always happen upon rebirth, but there is no reason why it can’t also happen in the middle of incarnation, should you be willing to put in sufficient effort, as in gargantuan effort (like really gargantuan effort!) Are you sure you’d be prepared to come away feeling overly zestful, embodied, and inextricably connected? How exhausting that could turn out to be to have to wake up to day after day! Whatever you do, don’t get so carried away that you end up throwing out the aspects of your Self that you currently enjoy.

As usual, the best means of escape is to head right into the center of the mêlée, wading directly into whatever you most want to avoid confronting. The lessons that can free you are buried directly under where parasites are currently feeding energy off of you. Detach the parasites, clearing away their disruption of your natural energy flow, and the lessons you want to learn will be right there in front of you, unobstructed. As for how to clear away parasites and curses, the options are endless. Bodywork, prayer, dream journaling, banishing rituals, psychotherapy, will training, energy visualization, laying on of hands, exorcism, trance work, and so many other methods all can do the job. I'm thinking of someone I know who is adamant that had his grandfather not smoked, he would still be alive, despite the fact that the man died at well over a hundred, more than 20 years ago. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The entire thrust of Western civilization -- Faustian civilization, as Spengler called it -- is a passion for linear extension into infinity. We're obsessed with straight lines; we're the only culture in history ever to make linear perspective central to our art, until even that became too much of a limit; we dreamed of sailing over oceans until we ran out of new lands to discover, and then started dreaming of space travel. Death -- the ultimate limit for the personality -- is unbearable for such a mindset, and all kinds of absurd belief systems have grown up to let people pretend that they don't have to die. Is it insane? You bet -- and it's one of the reasons I've come to think that Faustian civilization won't survive long.

comments.title: Impactation and Polarity
comments.content:
Hi John Micheal,

I'm currently working my way through Dion Fortunes chapters on Impactation and Polarity and I have to say that they are - even assisted by your commentary - much heavier going and much more opaque than most of the book that came before. It may well be that I'm far off the mark and therefore, If I may, I'd like to present a few of the results of my musings and I would be glad if you could comment.

1) After reading a passage of the Jung-Pauli letter exchange* it occurred to me that divination could be seen as an act of impactation. There are two partners - 1) on the material plane the person that performs the physical act of drawing sticks, cards, etc. and 2) on a higher plane the spiritual power that's behind the oracle. One could assign 1+ as the desire to understand the higher aspects of the situation that is subjected to divination, 1- could be the physical act of performing a reading; 2- is the part of the symbolic aspect that pushes toward manifestation in form of a certain reading and 2+ is what drives upwards to the higher causes behind the situation. The "swapping of places" that forms the circuit / the symbol would then be caused by the intention of the diviner, causing the symbolic representation of the situation to descend all the way down into the physical alignment of the sticks, cards, etc. and the material desire to know to ascend towards the understanding of the driving forces behind it.

2) Polarity magic, the bar of impactation and the symbol of the Hexagram - here it may well be that I am very far off from what Fortune intends to say - but anyway. I'll take the example of a priest and congregation: Fortune writes about a duality and the correspondence on a higher plane that form the basis of the Hexagram. In this way, Priest and congregation could form the lower left and right points of the hexagram. The respective symbolic correspondences (could one say the archetypal priest, the archetypal congregation?) would then form the upper left and right points. Energy is transferred from congregation to priest (say from right to left) and is then send "upward" to the divine, i.e. towards the upper tip of the hexagram. Which then descends to the benefit of the congregation and completes the triangle. One could draw a bar of impactation from left to right where the barrier between the lower and the higher plane is located, but I am not at all sure if this is the way it is meant (although in previous chapters Fortune seems to suggest this). That is because if you draw the second triangle (where the archetypal images exchange energy and descend towards manifestation of the (divine?) image of priest and congregation (i.e. a church!?) a lot of barriers come into being, not necessarily between two planes. For example, the connection between congregation and priest would be intersected twice by the descending triangle which makes sense since as you said the energies between the horizontal poles shouldn't earth out in a more mundane fashion.

Ok, I think that's enough for now. Thank you in advance for taking the time to read what I have written!
Cheers!

---
* One small part of a long letter Jung wrote to Pauli did the trick (translation and remarks by me): "It is not clear what exactly is meant by 'relativity of mass'. In the randomness of the movement of dices a psychological arrangement [remark: analogous to the experimental setup of a (quantum)physical experiment] is created. Has the modification its cause in the dices becoming heavier or lighter or in their speed being accelerated or slowed down? The limits of probability are transcended by the mass (i.e. the dice) in the same way as the 'knowledge' of the test person gains improbability [remark: most likely improbability in the sense of improbable to know under a materialistic paradigm.] I seek the explanation in the mysterious nature of the archetype, which temporarily abrogates the permanence of the causal principle and through contingency assimilates a physical and a psychological process into each other. One can describe this synchronistic event as a property of the psyche or of mass. In the first case, the psyche would bewitch mass, in the latter it would be mass that bewitches the psyche. It is therefore more probable that both share the same property, that both share a contingency in the background and - not caring for their own causal determination - intermingle with each other. Another possibility is that neither mass nor psyche have such a property but that there is a third factor, to which this property has to be ascribed; a factor which belongs to the realm of the psyche and which can be observed from a psychological point of view - namely the (psychoid) archetype, which through its habitual uncertainty and its 'transgressivity' suddenly (in a so called numinous moment) assimilates two incommensurable causal processes into each other and creates a common field of stresses (?) or makes them both 'radioactive' (?)." [remark: field and radioactivity are physical metaphors to psychological processes which seem to frequently occur in Pauli's dreams and are used in the letters by both Jung and Pauli].

I can't help thinking that it would have been utterly fascinating if Pauli and Jung have had access to the CosDoc...

A while back I attended a reading in my school. The author read a part of his book and explained the background to the pupils (aged 14-16). He inquired about a former SS-"worker" at Auschwitz during WW2. The person in question, now well beyond 90 years old, was convicted by a German court a few years ago. During his time in Auschwitz he was responsible for the looting of personal good of detained Jews and transferring the money to Berlin on a regular basis. The author of the book described in great detail how the young SS-man came to Auschwitz genuinely not knowing what his duties would be and what was done there, the struggle and disgust against the excessive brutality, but also highlighted how he restrained from being transferred to another place of duty, for that meant he would have had to fight at the eastern front against the Soviets and in Auschwitz his live was rather pleasant with plenty of food and Schnaps.

The author, who presented all of this to the pupils was puzzled and disgusted by the fact that the SS-man complied with his orders and said that of course he was just a minor gear in the whole mechanism but believed that if the minor gears fail, the whole machine would crash. He ended with the usual plea that the students should stay vigilant.

At that moment something clicked for me. How on earth can you invoke the specter of the Nazi crimes at their peak and at the same time tell the pupils to be vigilant? I think the very big mistake the German society makes is to focus it's attention at the characteristics of the final disaster and believe that being vigilant means watching out for early signs of the same characteristics and instantly beat them to oblivion. We are playing whack-a-mole in the far-right corner, waiting for an unfriendly guy with a funny mustache to show up and beat him down instantly. The point we are missing is that there have been years, no decades or even longer periods of time when the developments took place that lead to the final disaster. Look at what happened 30 years and even more before the Nazis raise to power, there you might find something that is worth to be vigilant against and there you might still have a chance to change the course of things.

But a clueless young bloke or average intelligence who knows little about the world, fed since early childhood with the propaganda of his time on his way to Auschwitz to fill a place in a violent environment filled with weapons, atrocities and alpha-males and in the background of his mind the specter of possibly being sent to his own death should he not comply - to expect of him to be the small gear that makes the machine stop?

If the approaching fist is only 1/10 inch away from your face, it's too late to start a block.

I think people fail to notice what is gradually becoming of them is because they are incapable of seeing the early motions in their own and the collective psyche - just like an untrained person possibly fails to notice that he is walking into a dangerous situation (at an early stage) or (at a later stage) he is about to be punched in the face very soon. And this is possible, because most people are in a dreamlike state where they are merely observers of the effects of subconscious forces moving their minds and society as a whole and as such the forces are able to act out according to their nature. Just like when in your dreams your subconscious takes control of your imagination with you being the observer but without direct awareness of the underlying forces and even more rarely in control.

C.G. Jung's essay Wotan - How was Jung able to predict the broad course of things? Because he at least partly understood the subconscious forces that move individuals and societies. Therefore he was able to predict things but at a rather late stage - when you have already moved into a dangerous situation but might still be able to block the punch. Hence, I think, Jung's hope that the archetypal Wotan would play out by his mantic characteristics rather than by war frenzy, but no talking about a possibly putting the archetype down.

It might be not possible to built a stable society which largely consists of individuals who have the necessary level of awareness. Which explains the need for the occasional saint, prophet, Buddha, beings who have developed such a level of awareness that they are able to guide people and societies through their dreamlike state in a constructive way. Most people might not be aware of what happens, but they feel that the effect of what happens on a subtler level is something positive, just like we feel happy when we have experienced a good dream.

If you have made it this far, thank you for reading :-) I hope it was ok that I jumped in here with my thoughts on the matter.

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

comments.title: Mission Tactics and New Thought, Shinto, Silva Mind Control, Ragnarok, and DA Nwyfre Exercises
comments.content:
Good Evening,

To Share: 1. I wasn't able to post much last week, but while reading comments after the post was closed, I noted Mocha Amphibious Moose's comment about reading military doctrine, and especially the concept of "Commander's Intent" and expressing it in short, clear, present-tense statements. JMG responded that it sounds like there was some New Thought influence there, which is fascinating. For what it's worth, I wanted to add that much of the military's doctrine/advice/stated approach to things is actually /very/ good, especially in the area of "how to be a good leader." The trouble is that there are a lot of cultural, political, and institutional forces pushing in, shall we say, less good ways, with the end result that the actual military doesn't function as much like it says it should as anyone would hope (including the fact that most missions do not have particularly short or clear commander's intent statements, and "delegation" often has far less autonomy than it really should). As for where this came from, the standard history given is that "Mission Tactics" (commander has a clear intent, then delegates as much as he can to lower-level leaders) were developed by the Germans at the tail-end of WW1, and played a big part in the late war tactical successes that led to the "stabbed in the back" narrative taken advantage of by certain political leaders down the road. Anyway, the Allies all saw the on-the-ground effectiveness of this approach and began training their armies in the same way, and these days it's standard doctrine in most militaries. To bring things back to the "magic" part of Monday, I'd simply note that this means that this approach was "the hot new thing" in the 20s and 30s, and that motivated, get-things-done guys tend to be drawn to the military, so the timing and subset of the population involved make the idea that the codification and spread of the approach incorporated some New Thought DNA wouldn't surprise me at all.

2. Another carryover from last week - an anonymous commenter asked for resources on Shinto. Anonymous (or anyone else), if you're reading, after a similar question a while back, I put together this roundup that might be helpful: https://sanepolytheism.dreamwidth.org/3448.html

3. Man, I was really missing out last week! Another anonymous commenter asked about the Silva Mind Control Method - something like 5-10 years ago, I followed the exercises given in the main book for, oh, several months, I think? This was before I took magic seriously as anything other than "interacting with my subconscious." So, I gave it a decent go, but didn't work through it with the discipline I'm trying to bring to such systems now. From what I remember, I think that the system's emphasis on a single state (I can't remember the term they use) as the only way to then be able to do psychic acts is maybe overly reductive. That being said, the exercises did seem to be an effective way to induce that state, and it was conducive to pretty vivid, dream-like visualization, at least if you could manage to not fall asleep during it! As you progress, the system also has a very strong emphasis on that visualization, and it stresses putting that visualization "outside" of your head/eyelids, on an imagined screen a few feet away from your body. I found this very difficult, as I have a hard enough time getting clear visualization when I do it the way that comes more naturally ("in" my head), but maybe persisting with it has certain benefits. My only other comment, with hindsight, is that one of the things that appealed to me about the approach back then, no insistence on non-physical spiritual realities, and no overtly religious elements, I might these days see as a drawback. If I were going to pursue it again, it would be in the context of also doing some kind of banishing ritual and regular prayer.

4. I've kept on with my thoughts on Maria Kvilhaug's The Seed of Yggrdrasill, this time covering Ragnarok and what comes after: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_the_seed_of_yggdrasill_6_ragnarok_and_the_new_age

To Ask: Do you know of any compatibility issues or preferred sequencing for the Great Psychic Breath + Seven Nwyfre exercises given in Grade 4 of the Dolmen Arch and the Five Rites? I am a long-time reader starting in the early days of the ADR. First time poster. I deeply appreciate your systemic view of what’s going on and your intention to place everything that’s happening in a bigger context.

replies.title: Re: questions: re planes of manifestation
replies.content:
I've thought on your second question quite a bit. Thinking of life force flowing down through the planes from spirit down to physical manifestation, I posit that many forms of verbal mental and emotional abuse involve the abuser seeking to climb to a higher level violently and vicariously. By this I mean that the abuser wants to be in control but rather than working on their own thoughts, emotions, and ideals in order to gain greater power over their own physical situation, they imagine an ideal (that they cannot meet) and seek to become the thoughts and emotions of the other person(s). So then the abuser vicariously feels empowered, and in some way, this insertion into another persons process of manifestation allows them to vampirically benefit from the victim's life force. (If you know anything about network security, this resembles a man-in-the-middle attack.) In extreme cases, the victim begins to shift their own connection to higher planes to the abuser giving the abuser a god-complex and divorcing the abused from their spiritual center.

Obviously this is all from my experience (and that of others close to me) and from my meditations on those experiences; one person's perspective and your mileage may vary.

replies.title: Re: questions: re planes of manifestation

comments.title: Lust for result
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

Thank you as always for this space and for your responses
to all of the questions you receive from your readership.

I have noticed that any magic that I do tends to manifest when my strong desire for it to take effect has disappeared. From my former Buddhist background this makes sense - attachment is suffering the Buddha says, and there is a definite sense of discomfort that comes from realising that one does not have the thing that one has done magic for.

My question is what is the best way to remove ones "lust for result" (as one author called it) after generating and experiencing the strong desire for the result that is the impetus for the magic in the first place?

Is this an experience (manifestation after letting go) you recognise in your own practice? That is, it us a universal law of magic?

Thanks,

P

replies.title: Re: Lust for result
replies.content:
That's one of the most widely recognized laws in magic. The more emotionally attached you are to a given outcome, the less effective your magic will be. That's partly because the lust of result leads you to focus as much on the fact that you don't have what you want as on getting the thing you want, and partly because the higher a plane you can work from, the more effective your magic will be -- and attraction and repulsion are both astral phenomena, thus on a fairly low level. (Repulsion is lower than attraction, which is why magic motivated by love is more powerful than magic motivated by hate; magic motivated by clear conscious intention, without emotional involvement, is of course more powerful still.)

It takes a lot of work to get past the craving for results. Daily meditation is a potent tool for this, and so is simple experience -- the more magic you practice, especially if you put a lot of work into rituals for spiritual development, the easier it becomes to set attraction and repulsion aside and attune to the pure intention itself.

comments.title: Chakras as resonators?
comments.content:
Esteemed JMG and all!

R.J. Stewart made a fascinating comment in his book "The Sphere of Art" about the nature of chakras, and I'm asking here if anyone knows anything more about this.

(Some context: Stewart, like our illustrious JMG, cautions people not to equate the body areas used in their specific esoteric exercises with chakras. Stewart's comments here are part of his argument against mixing Westernized and de-contextualized ideas of chakra-working into Western esoteric work.)

Stewart says that a chakra "was originally (and, in Eastern traditions, still is) regarded as a tympanum or responsive organ upon which spirit beings could resonate and so interact with humanity. The awakening of these subtle organs of communication and communication was the task of deities and spirits, not of human solipsism. ... Chakras are...organs of reception and communication." (p. 52)

Considering what I wrote a couple weeks ago about the perception that an important avenue of interaction and communication on the spiritual plane occurs through resonance, I find this a very intriguing statement, and would like to learn more about it!

Best wishes to all,
Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Chakras as resonators?
replies.content:
Stewart is always well worth studying and reading. Here he's following one broad current of Asian esoteric teaching; I'm not sure what his specific sources are, but if you contact him I'm sure he'd be glad to tell you.

That's only one of several traditions of Asian origin regarding the chakras, however. In influential traditions of laya yoga and kriya yoga, by contrast, the chakras are awakened by the individual practitioner and the main effects sought from them are not communicative in nature. Stewart is of course right that clumsy borrowings of half-understood and decontextualized chakra work into Western esoteric practices are a bad idea; it's for this reason that the most effective Western systems of work with energy centers, such as the Middle Pillar exercise or the system of seven vortices I discuss in my recent book The Secret of the Five Rites, don't copy the chakra system verbatim but use their own sets of energy centers for their own specific purposes.

comments.title: Buddism
comments.content:
Hello John.

I don't know if you have had experience with something like this but I prefer to ask a sage.

I am very interested in Buddhism however it is extremely distant for my culture (I come from Eastern Europe). Additionally, I am somewhat repulsed by the issue of , "destruction of the self" in it, a concept that seems foreign to the Western mind. I know that the sense of "who I am" is not the same as "I" but further, the Buddhist unification with everything through the annihilation of the "self" seems to me to be something that is too...I don't know, extreme? It seems to me that the spiritual paths of the West are more oriented towards uniting with all and preserving the ,,I" Like alchemists discovering the philosopher's stone, they did not stop being themselves but became a better version of themselves. Perhaps I am making a thought error somewhere, what is your opinion JMG?

replies.title: Re: Buddism
replies.content:
Buddhism started out in ancient India as a profoundly pessimistic teaching; the Buddha came to the conclusion that existence was fundamentally a miserable state, and worked out a way to stop existing. (When I recently started reading Thomas Ligotti's The Conspiracy Against the Human Race, a popular modern work of world-hating pessimism, I was powerfully reminded of the early Buddhist suttas.) It was a refuge for world-weary sophisticates in the twilight years of India's ancient civilization. As that civilization unraveled, though, Buddhism got picked up by people of more optimistic inclinations and massively reworked. All of a sudden, as the Mahayana tradition hit its stride, the self you were getting rid of became the false self that keeps you from experiencing your own Buddha nature; the world of illusion, Samsara, turned out to be secretly identical with Nirvana; and the ideal of snuffing out the self to avoid suffering gave way to a dive into infinite bliss in Buddha-realms.

It's really quite a remarkable story. What this means in practice is that if you want to get into Buddhism, by all means do so; given your interests, you might consider one of the Mahayana, Mantrayana, or Vajrayana schools.

replies.title: Re: Buddism
replies.content:
"Buddhism started out in ancient India as a profoundly pessimistic teaching; the Buddha came to the conclusion that existence was fundamentally a miserable state, and worked out a way to stop existing."

I've always wondered about something: does the Buddha's teaching actually work for this goal? I've always wondered if it was possible to use spiritual practices to destroy oneself like this; and if it is, whether or not human beings can do it...

replies.title: Re: Buddism
replies.content: It seems very unlikely to me, though it certainly kept him from feeling anything while he was alive.

replies.title: Re: Buddism
replies.content: That sounds like a recipe for some very unpleasant future lives.

replies.title: Re: Buddism
replies.content: It certainly doesn't strike me as a good idea. Fortunately most Buddhists ever since have had the great good sense to redefine nirvana until it no longer means what the word does in fact mean, and pursue spiritual development rather than personal annihilation.

replies.title: Re: Buddism
replies.content: Does this mean that the classic image and popular statue of laughing happy Buddha, and the stories of a whimsical and playfully teasing Buddha, depict a character the actual ascetic meditator of Nirvana would not have recognized?

replies.title: Re: Buddism
replies.content:
This strikes me as a slightly tendentious reading. Buddhism seems more like a deconstructionist/reformation reaction against the literalisation of the vedic/upanishads tradition in the forms of elaborate ritualisation and scholasticism of the priestly parts of the society.

Theravada buddhism did the same thing back to buddhism, whereas the buddha was very much about "appropriate vehicle" for appropriate person, which includes the tradition that is most appropriate to your spiritual unfolding, and fundamentally an anti-doctrinal view. All is mind = no mind.

As a child I used to have bad stomach aches. I grew without any religion or spirituality in the family, but because of my stomach pains I one day spontaneously prayed to God to help me. replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
2) I worry about this too, but even if it were self-perpetuating, that wouldn't have to mean that it were contingent/arbitrary/path-dependent or parasitic in an overall sense.

I suspect that it's analogous to languages. Languages have a lot of seemingly contingent/arbitrary/path-dependent qualities and details, and languages are one of the focus aspects of contention and power struggles (as the old saying goes, "a language is a dialect with an army and a navy"). But, even though different actors stood to gain or lose different advantages and disadvantages when a language acquired some details over others, and even though there may have been many generations of selection for winners learning to put their thumb on the scales for which direction a language takes, still, these "meaningless" traces left by the relative advantages and disadvantages in these selfish struggles are normally small, relative to the much larger and more meaningful traces left by how languages are shaped by their users to solve problems that could not be solved if language were entirely absent.

(I say "seemingly" arbitrary details, because probably every detail of everything in the world we experience is touched by a little bit of calculation of alternative timelines and potential combinations of details, such that the worlds that get chosen to be experienced end up being more meaningful to something -- probably mostly something outside the world. But that doesn't mean the extra-worldly meaningfulness of the ways that the meanings in a language was skewed by power interests is beneficial to the actual physical organisms inside the world, trying to use the meanings within the language itself. Also, this invisible extraworldly calculation would also be the underlying mechanism for how astrology would work, whereas the underlying mechanism for how language works involves more prosaic things like "game-theoretic equilibrium calculation" and "multi-agent learning". So the fact that I'm bringing up this hypothesis about an invisible extraworldly calculation, to qualify my claim about arbitrariness, unfortunately confuses the levels of the analogy.)

Similarly, which corporations and firms are on top of the heap at any given time is arbitrary and the details of those firms are contingent, but the economic problems that they solve are somewhat inherent to the material situation and would not have been solved without some such form of organization, and also the fact that those are the firms that survived the culling of market discipline is not to be trifled with.

I assume that the situation with astrology (and with other systems of archetypally invested symbolism, such as pantheons) is similar: the details are contingent, but the problems the "arbitrary" details are configured to solve are not themselves arbitrary. (When it's just humans doing the arbitrary details, we call it "construction" or "social construction"; when there's another side co-operating, we call it "co-construction". JMG has mentioned a framing in which divine beings accept human offerings of astral forms -- imagery and associations.) Maybe it's possible to gradually shape symbol systems that have fallen into fewer traps, by understanding the principles that create the traps, and that can be part of our contribution to the co-construction; but replacing entire systems may be beyond our competency -- we haven't had much luck with artificial constructed languages, whether because we constructed them wrong or because it was infeasible to get traction, and something like astrology probably involves even more co-constructing influences we aren't seeing than language does.

There isn't good theory about how that would ever all relate to physics, but if you have a background in physics, computer science, philosophy, and game theory, a reasonable basis of speculation about the invisible extraworldly calculation, that might be powering divination and shaping the details of astrological symbolism, might start from a long program of study in which the following elements of science and scientific speculation would find a place:

"The Capricious, Actively Evasive, Unsustainable Nature of Psi: A Summary and Hypotheses", by James E. Kennedy: https://jeksite.com/psi/jp03.htm

Stephen Wolfram's speculations about "branchial space", in which quantum physics is shown to be plausibly a high-level consequence of certain kinds of very low-level network-rewriting automata connecting different worlds by possible ways in which the worlds could be rewritten into each other, and his extension of these speculations to a "rulial space". The version of the "rulial space" speculation that I find noteworthy is that, in rulial space, not only do worlds following the same rules with slightly different histories of microphysical internal events bleed into each other as in regular quantum "branchial space", but worlds with slightly different entire sets of rules they'd "always" been following also bleed into each other. (In an occult context, it might be important that what it means for two sets of rules to be "slightly different", with respect to any single rule, probably depends on how often that rule comes up, and on how reliably the outcome has historically gone one way rather than another in situations where the rule would have come up. This has big suggestive parallels to Dion Fortune's image of "tracks in space". This interaction between physical rules and physical details also contains an element where the details can affect the rules, at least the local rules in that branch of history going forward; this feature resembles the "return stroke" in Kabbalistic cosmology.) Unfortunately I know of no short introduction to this. Wolfram is probably wrong that whatever's going on is literally network-rewriting, but it could very well be something that's practically equivalent, and he's doing the work and I'm not, so I shouldn't quibble with his choice of conceptual framing too much.

I think small variables in rulial state would probably correspond to weak hard-to-detect fields in quantum field theory, and higher-planar phenomena in occult cosmology. I'm not sure how these variations would relate to ordinary space-time structure, except that variables that were "shallower" in the tree of rules might have more spatially localized variation than variables that were "deeper", and so might correspond to lower planes. (It is possible, for all I know, that the deeper rulial layers would maintain connections between quantum branches that were otherwise separated, thus corresponding to e.g. how the "causal" plane would be beyond time and possibility in the usual sense, but I would be surprised if such connections were more than a limited resource, that could not be used to exert very much general-purpose influence from one branch onto another without decohering.)

A variety of computer science concepts relating to formalizing possibility of sets of rules, and how different sets of rules might appear similar, interact, or simulate each other. Notably, the ideas of "emulation" and "computational universality", the idea of a "universal dovetailer", the idea of "probabilistic programming languages", the idea of "program equilibrium", and the whole range of strategies for trying to prove that two programs behave the same way or that one program can be made to do the same as another. Intuitions from these concepts should apply moderately well to the problem of understanding the relationships between subtly different sets of physical rules, and you may see further helpful cross-pollination with the hypothesis of the "mathematical multiverse". Wolfram seems to think that "homotopy type theory" will be important for understanding the implications of his physics paradigm, and it may also helpful as a source of intuitions here.

Sorry how that probably doesn't help! Even if the above were correct speculation, sometimes things are too complicated or hard to reason all that precisely about!

(This is all quite aside from speculation about the "simulation hypothesis", which I assume JMG considers distasteful. But if even we can conceive of things like astrological correspondences and rulial space and program equilibria, can we be sure that a hypothetical state of greater technology wouldn't include people who could invent imagined supernatural cosmologies like that, automated or semi-automated for the subjects of a simulation or an immersive fiction to experience, perhaps for some artistic purpose? That whole sphere of "artistic purposes" is another place where that kind of extra-worldly calculation could be coming from.)

comments.title: Jung
comments.content: A quick question, can anyone recommend three good books to get started with Carl Jung’s teachings?

replies.title: Re: Jung
replies.content: I'd recommend Man and His Symbols, the visionary autobiography Memories, Dreams, Reflections, and then straight into Symbols of Transformation.

replies.title: Re: Jung
replies.content: Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Jung
replies.content:
If you find yourself wanting to supplement with other writers, you might find Jordan Peterson's Maps of Meaning and The Origin and History of Consciousness by Erich Neumann helpful.

Peterson's book is not explicitly about Jung, but Jung is clearly the single biggest influence there, and most of what it's about is a simplification/rationalization of the archetypes. His work is more systematized than Jung's (and also less complex, subtle, and nuanced), but it's still fairly sprawling and can be a bit much to take in - I wrote up a summary to try to help with that, in case that's helpful: https://jpowellrussell.com/#jordan_peterson_s_framework_for_myth

Neumann was a student of Jung's and Jung said of his book that he did a better job of stating everything as a whole than he had been able to, since he was coming up with it along the way, whereas Neumann could look at it more-or-less fully formed. Again, the chief benefit is also the chief drawback: it's more rational and systematized, which means some of the nuance and specific details get left out.

Not sure if these are the direction you want to go, but if so, I hope they help.

Cheers,
Jeff

What tack do you take, if any, if you suspect you remember something about a previous life?

I've been watching some old video lectures on the history of ancient Egypt. After one of them, I felt suddenly, urgently sleepy. I fell asleep right there on the couch and dreamed that I had a certain name and occupation in ancient Egypt. It felt like the dream was trying to pound those two facts into my head. I woke up with that name practically ringing in my ears, googled, and found out that the name (which I don't think I'd heard before) was extremely common in ancient Egypt. I might as well have been "Dave" or "Bob".

My first thoughts were, "Consider why you might be becoming aware of this now, and consult an oracle." The "why now" seems to be simply that I was watching a lecture on the time period and had something joggled. If there's any more to it, I don't know it; if this is in some more meaningful way the right time for this to come to my awareness, I don't see why. I've asked the I Ching for its perspective; its answer was 42.2 ("Increase", "Help From Above") changing into 61 ("Inner Truth", "Sincerity In the Center").

"Extraordinary benefits accrue from outside your immediate awareness" is one interpretation of 42.2. Which is great, but despite the fact that this dream did everything short of picking me up and shaking me, I don't feel transformed in any way or anything like that. It feels like having a sudden vivid memory about where I went to elementary school. If understanding myself as a likely reincarnation of Egyptian Bob is an extraordinary benefit, I'm not sure what that is, but I'll meditate on it.

I'd welcome guidance. Thank you!

-- Magenta Wimpy Wombat

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Write down everything you remember, put it away somewhere, and then set the matter aside. If it's a real memory, other details will start to surface.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Bobby Dave," 42.2 says why you're so blessed: "the king" (a high authority, connecting heaven and earth) "introduces him to the emperor" (an even higher authority).

This shows that there is another level of spiritual advancement here for you. The introduction will be made on your behalf. You don't have to go pester the emperor, or figure out the right thing to say to the king. It will happen for you.

In 61, the inner truth is so obvious that even pigs and fish find themselves wandering along in its direction. The inner truth is so compelling that it provides grace and kindness to temper the harshness of justice. "The high ruler provides analysis that stays the death penalty."

If there is some karmic burden from that past life, it will be lifted from you by the inner truth which you are introduced to.

Good things happening, and they're not up to what you can figure out or make happen. Enjoy the experience!

Christopher from California

comments.title: Spiritual emergence
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thank you as always for this space.

Earlier this year, I completed the full sequence of OSA materials, and I’m just winding up work on the final grade of the Dolmen Arch.

I’ve been experiencing some challenging things.

A couple of months ago, I began to have occasional prophetic dreams. My foresights were nothing spectacular, just circumstantial yet highly specific and accurate pieces of information that it would have been very unlikely to have known in advance.

I then started to experience gentle altered states of consciousness most mornings when I would be in the garden. This was quite beautiful and I felt a deepening connection to nature.

Over the past three weeks, this has intensified significantly and I am now in a permanent altered and ‘heightened’ state of consciousness, feeling profound connection with the spiritual force of nature.

Some other things occurring are waves of anxiety in my body, feelings of electricity in my hands, an intense pressure in my brow centre that sometimes leads to headaches, and difficulty sleeping.

I realise that these ‘symptoms’ correlate with what is often called kundalini awakening or spiritual emergence / emergency.

I feel that I am in a stage of purification, uprooting all aspects of my thoughts, beliefs and behaviours which distract me from truly following my Awen. An example of this purification is a deep sadness and emotional pain I feel if I make a cruel, throwaway remark about somebody in casual conversation, and an immediate resolve to refrain from such ‘useless’ talk.

My current daily practice is:

Affirmations
Contemplative reading
Prayer
Complete SOP to awaken my individuality
Meditation
Ogham divination
Game of the Cantrefs
Healing passes
Nwyfre breathing exercises
Individuality (hot air balloon) visualisation
Strengthening the enaid and cosmic will visualisations (both from DA final grade)
Some form of breath practice from the OSA

I have two questions:

Have you encountered anything like this before in students of the Dolmen Arch as they earnestly pursue the awakening of the individuality?

In such a situation, would you recommend continuing a daily round of practice like I’ve described, or might it be better to pare things back until this expanded state of consciousness (and accompanying waves of anxiety) calm down?

Thank you for any advice you may offer.

Gavin.

replies.title: Re: Spiritual emergence
replies.content:
1) This is the fast version of what the DA system is supposed to do. Some people get it in a rush, some people get it more gradually. Anyone who works hard at the system will get it sooner or later.

2) As long as you're comfortable with it, maintain the practices at their current level -- though I don't recommend adding anything else just now! If it becomes uncomfortable, back off somewhat. SoP, meditation, and divination daily are the minimum, but you can certainly drop down to that if you need to. So long as you can deal with your current experiences, though, I'd recommend keeping at it.

replies.title: Re: Spiritual emergence
replies.content:
Going through a K awakening all year and I would recommend paring back spiritual practices if it gets too intense.

Also try to get get enough sleep (take supplements if needed), and focus on grounding energy.

comments.content:
In Sane Occultism, Dion Fortune said "....occult systems transplanted to America. They will never strike their roots there, but remain superficial and academic." She suggested they try to make use of the land itself, but noted that those trained in European systems would have a rough time of it until they learned to stop importing systems from the Old World and "go back along the line of its own tradition, picking up aboriginal contacts, and daring to bend them to its own evolutionary purposes." Which sounds to me a lot like shaking off the European pseudomorphasis by another name. What's your take on this? I think there's a lot of truth to what she's saying, but it's not coming very vlear.

Then she ends by looking at Latin America for the source of those contacts. I feel uneasy about that, even though the Southwest is probably well among that road. Partly because we are basically North Americans, and partly because a lot of those Latin American gods were a bloodthirsty lot.Again, your take?

The Grey Badger

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Dion Fortune never visited America. I've noticed that a lot of British people who've never been here have no idea how huge this country is or how many different nations and regions are part of it. (My favorite example is the British couple a friend of mine met in Portland who wanted to drive south to Los Angeles that morning, have lunch there, then go on to New Orleans to get a hotel for the night. They literally had no idea how absurd that plan was.)

The Mayan (or, originally, Olmec) contacts are relevant to Mexico and Central America, and to the American Southwest; they don't extend beyond that. Here along the east coast there are older Atlantean contacts that can be picked up; the Norse contacts are also remarkably strong -- I get the impression the Norse may have had more of a presence along the Atlantic coast than current historians think. The Pacific Northwest has a completely different set of contacts, which resonate with other North Pacific cultures -- probably Lemurian in origin ultimately, though mediated through other cultures. So it's not as simple as she thought.

comments.title: Who is this?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I've recently become aware of something that I now believe I've been experiencing all my life. Every time I look in a mirror (to shave or whatever), I get this feeling of not knowing who the person in the mirror is. As I have aged, this has gotten more pronounced to the point where I've been actually noticing it recently. I recognize the face, but I'm always surprised that I'm seeing this face and not another's. Looking back in memory, I now believe I've been this way for a long time, perhaps always. What do you think?

replies.title: Re: Who is this?
replies.content:
My guess, though it's only a guess, is that you had too little time between your last incarnation and this one -- given the sheer number of human bodies that need to be filled with souls these days, that's very common. Thus you still have subconscious memories of your last body, and it's always a bit of a surprise when you find yourself in this one.

I suspect that's a lot of what's behind gender dysphoria these days, for that matter.
